References

Earth Processes · Week 1

Maps, Location & Cartographers

Understanding Peru's place on Earth through the science and history of mapping — from Inca roads to colonial atlases to satellite imagery.

Four Maps of Peru

Each map reveals a different layer of Peru's geographic story — ancient, colonial, modern, and physical.

MAP 1

The Inca Road Network — Qhapaq Ñan (Ancient Routes)

This map shows the extent of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu) at its peak around 1530 CE, and the Qhapaq Ñan — the royal road network that stretched over 40,000 km across western South America. The roads connected Cusco (the Inca capital in modern-day Peru) with territories reaching as far north as modern Colombia and as far south as central Chile. This network enabled military movement, trade, communication by runners (chasquis), and administrative control over 12 million people. Peru formed the heart of this empire, making it one of the most sophisticated road systems in the ancient world.

Source: Wikimedia Commons. "Inca Empire South America." Public domain.

MAP 2

Continental Position & Latitudinal Range

This locator map illustrates Peru's position on the western coast of South America, flanked by Ecuador and Colombia to the north, Brazil and Bolivia to the east, and Chile to the south. Peru's latitudinal range spans approximately 0°01'S to 18°21'S — entirely within the Southern Hemisphere and the tropical/subtropical zones. This latitudinal range is why Peru experiences both equatorial rainforest in the Amazon basin and arid desert along the coast (influenced by the cold Humboldt Current). Peru also spans from sea level (Pacific coast) to over 6,700 m (Huascarán), giving it one of the greatest altitudinal ranges on Earth.

Source: Wikimedia Commons. "Peru in South America." CC BY-SA 3.0.

MAP 3

Modern Political Map — Mercator Projection

This modern interactive map (rendered using OpenStreetMap data in the Mercator projection) shows Peru's current political boundaries, major cities, rivers, and road infrastructure. The Mercator projection is a cylindrical projection developed by Gerardus Mercator in 1569; it preserves angles and shapes of small areas (making it ideal for navigation), but distorts size at higher latitudes. Because Peru is near the equator (~0°–18°S), Mercator distortion is relatively minimal here compared to how it would appear for countries near the poles. The map reveals Peru's three major geographic regions: the narrow coastal strip (Costa), the Andes highlands (Sierra), and the vast Amazon basin (Selva).

Source: OpenStreetMap contributors. OpenStreetMap. CC BY-SA 2.0. Retrieved 2026.

MAP 4

Boundary Changes — Viceroyalty of Peru to Modern Republic

This map shows the Viceroyalty of Peru (1542–1821), the Spanish colonial territory that at its greatest extent covered most of South America — including present-day Peru, Bolivia, Chile, Argentina, and Paraguay. When Peru declared independence in 1821, the territory was dramatically reduced through border negotiations, the War of the Pacific (1879–1884) — in which Peru lost its southernmost Tarapacá region to Chile — and treaties with neighboring nations through the 20th century. Peru's current borders were largely finalized by the Peruvian-Ecuadorian border settlement in 1998 (Treaty of Brasília). Comparing the viceroyalty extent to the modern republic illustrates how profoundly political borders reshape geographic identity.

Source: Wikimedia Commons. "Virreinato del Perú." CC BY-SA 3.0.

Geographic Questions

Written answers to four key questions about Peru's geography, history, and cartographic legacy.

1

On which continent and in what general area is Peru located?

Peru is located on the continent of South America, positioned along the west-central coast of the continent. It borders the Pacific Ocean to the west, placing it in the western portion of South America. Regionally, Peru occupies a central position between the Andean countries of the north (Ecuador and Colombia) and the Southern Cone nations (Chile). The country spans three dramatically different geographic zones: the coastal desert (Costa) hugging the Pacific, the Andes mountain range (Sierra) running north-to-south through the interior, and the Amazon rainforest basin (Selva) covering roughly 60% of the country's total land area to the east. Lima, the capital city, sits on the Pacific coast at approximately 12°S, 77°W.

2

What is the latitudinal range of Peru?

Peru's latitudinal range spans from approximately 0°01'48" South at its northernmost point (near the Putumayo River, on the border with Colombia) to 18°21'03" South at its southernmost point (near the Atacama region, on the border with Chile and Bolivia). This gives Peru a total latitudinal extent of roughly 18 degrees of latitude, entirely within the Southern Hemisphere.

This range places Peru within the tropical and subtropical zones. The equator passes very close to Peru's northern tip, which is why the Amazon basin in the northeast has a true equatorial climate with high rainfall year-round. As you move south, the influence of the cold Humboldt (Peru) Current along the coast creates a paradoxical hyperarid desert (the Atacama/Sechura Desert) at latitudes that would otherwise be tropical. The Andes further complicate the climate by creating distinct altitudinal zones within the same latitudinal band.

3

During what time periods or years has Peru existed?

"Peru" as a geographic and political concept has evolved significantly across millennia. Here is a summary of the major periods:

Pre-Columbian Era (~3000 BCE – 1532 CE)

The territory of modern Peru was home to some of the oldest civilizations in the Americas. The Norte Chico (Caral) civilization (~3000–1800 BCE) is one of the earliest in the world. Over millennia, cultures including the Chavín, Nazca, Moche, Tiwanaku, and Wari flourished. The Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu) unified the region from approximately 1438 CE under the rule of Pachacuti, reaching its peak territorial extent (~1527) before Spanish contact.

Spanish Colonial Era (1532 – 1821)

Francisco Pizarro led the Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire beginning in 1532. The Viceroyalty of Peru was formally established in 1542, making Lima one of the most important capitals in the Spanish colonial world. The viceroyalty administered most of Spanish South America at its height.

Republic of Peru (July 28, 1821 – Present)

Peruvian independence was declared on July 28, 1821, by General José de San Martín. Military independence was consolidated by Simón Bolívar at the Battles of Junín and Ayacucho (1824). Peru has existed as a sovereign republic for over 200 years, though with multiple constitutional revisions and political changes, including a period of military government (1968–1980).

4

Famous cartographers from or who influenced mapping in Peru

Several cartographers played defining roles in how Peru was mapped and understood by the wider world. Two stand out for their lasting impact:

Mapa Geográfico de América Meridional (1775)

Juan de la Cruz Cano y Olmedilla (1734–1790)

A Spanish cartographer and engraver, Juan de la Cruz Cano y Olmedilla created the Mapa Geográfico de América Meridional in 1775 — considered one of the most accurate and detailed maps of South America of the 18th century. The map covered all of South America on eight large sheets and was commissioned by the Spanish Crown. It depicted Peru's geography with unprecedented precision, including river systems, mountain ranges, and colonial administrative divisions. The map was so strategically important that the Spanish government initially suppressed its distribution to prevent rivals from using it.

Biggest cartographic accomplishment: Synthesizing decades of expedition data — including reports from Jesuit missionaries and Spanish colonial administrators — into a single coherent, scientifically accurate representation of western South America, setting the standard for South American cartography for nearly a century.

Charles-Marie de La Condamine

Charles-Marie de La Condamine (1701–1774)

A French mathematician, geographer, and explorer, La Condamine led the French Geodesic Mission to Peru (1735–1744) — one of the most important scientific expeditions in history. The mission traveled to the Viceroyalty of Peru (present-day Ecuador and Peru) to measure an arc of the meridian near the equator, in order to determine whether Earth was flattened at the poles or the equator, resolving a major scientific debate of the era.

Biggest cartographic accomplishments: La Condamine produced detailed maps of the Amazon River basin after traveling its full length in 1743 — the first scientific survey of the river. He also introduced the use of quinine (extracted from Peru's cinchona tree) to Europe and brought back the first rubber samples. His geographic measurements directly contributed to defining the meter as a unit of length, making his work in Peru foundational to modern scientific measurement systems.

References (APA Format)

  • Central Intelligence Agency. (2025). Peru. The World Factbook. https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/peru/

  • D'Altroy, T. N. (2002). The Incas. Blackwell Publishers.

  • Harley, J. B., & Woodward, D. (Eds.). (1987). The history of cartography: Vol. 1. Cartography in prehistoric, ancient, and medieval Europe and the Mediterranean. University of Chicago Press.

  • La Condamine, C.-M. de. (1745). Relation abrégée d'un voyage fait dans l'intérieur de l'Amérique méridionale. Mémoires de l'Académie Royale des Sciences.

  • Multhauf, R. P. (1976). The French Geodesic Mission and the measurement of the Earth. Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, 120(3), 228–236.

  • OpenStreetMap contributors. (2026). OpenStreetMap [Map tiles]. https://www.openstreetmap.org

  • UNESCO. (2014). Qhapaq Ñan, Andean road system. World Heritage List. https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1459/

  • Wikimedia Foundation. (2025). Juan de la Cruz Cano y Olmedilla. Wikipedia. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Juan_de_la_Cruz_Cano_y_Olmedilla

  • Wikimedia Foundation. (2025). Viceroyalty of Peru. Wikipedia. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_Peru

  • AI Use Disclosure: Claude (Anthropic, 2026) was used as a research assistant to identify relevant cartographers and structure geographic information. All factual claims were verified against the primary and secondary sources listed above. Claude AI did not serve as a source itself.
